disease.name: Cutaneous Melanoma
disease.note: Most dangerous skin cancer arising from melanocytes. ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregular, Color variegated, Diameter >6mm, Evolving. In darker-skinned African populations, acral lentiginous melanoma (palms, soles, subungual) is more common and often diagnosed late due to low clinical suspicion. Subungual melanoma is frequently misdiagnosed as fungal infection or trauma. Any non-healing pigmented lesion on the foot warrants biopsy.
presentingComplaints: A mole that has changed shape, size or color
A new dark spot on the skin
A sore that does not heal
Dark spot under a fingernail or toenail
Dark spot on the palm or sole
Itching or bleeding from a mole

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Metastatic/Breslow >4mm -> SEVERE; in situ / thin <1mm -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice this mole or spot?
Has it changed in size, shape, or color?
Does it itch, bleed, or crust?
Do you have a family history of skin cancer or melanoma?
Have you had severe sunburns in the past?
Do you have fair skin that burns easily?
Do you have many moles or atypical moles?
Have you had a previous melanoma or skin cancer?
Is this lesion on the palm, sole, or under a nail?
Do you have any other dark spots that concern you?
Have you noticed any lumps in your armpit, groin, or neck?
Have you had unexplained weight loss, fever, or night sweats?
Do you work outdoors or have significant sun exposure?
Have you ever used tanning beds?
Was the mole present at birth or did it appear later?
When did you first notice this skin lesion?
Has the lesion changed in size, shape, or color over time?
Is the lesion itchy, bleeding, or crusting?
Do you have a personal history of melanoma or other skin cancer?
Do you have a family history of melanoma (parent, sibling, child)?
Have you had severe sunburns, especially as a child?
Do you have many moles (over 50) or unusual-looking moles?
Have you ever used tanning beds or sunlamps?
Do you have a history of organ transplantation or immunosuppression?
Have you had previous skin biopsies of suspicious lesions?
Do you have any pigmented lesions on your feet, palms, or under nails?
Have you noticed any new dark streaks in your nails?
Do you have HIV or any immunosuppressive condition?
Have you had dysplastic nevus syndrome or atypical mole syndrome?
Is there a personal or family history of pancreatic cancer?
Do you feel any lumps or swelling in armpit, groin, or neck?
Have you had unexplained weight loss, fatigue, or fevers?
What is your occupation? Do you work outdoors?
Do you perform regular self-skin examinations?
Have you been diagnosed with any other cancer previously?

examination_findings.finding: Asymmetrical lesion (one half unlike the other)
Irregular, notched, or blurred borders
Color variegation (multiple shades of brown, black, blue, red, white)
Diameter >6 mm (pencil eraser size)
Elevation or nodularity
Ulceration or bleeding
Satellite pigmented lesions nearby
In-transit metastases (between primary and nodes)
Palpable regional lymphadenopathy
Subungual pigmentation with Hutchinson's sign (pigment extending to nail fold)
Amelanotic lesion (non-pigmented, pink/red nodule)
Scar from previous partial biopsy
Systemic exam: hepatomegaly, respiratory findings for mets
Perform complete skin examination: scalp, face, trunk, limbs, palms, soles, nails, mucosa
Apply ABCDE criteria to each pigmented lesion
Identify the ugly duckling sign (lesion different from all others)
Examine palms and soles carefully (most common site in African patients)
Inspect all fingernails and toenails for pigmented bands
Assess for ulceration, bleeding, crusting, or erosion
Palpate for nodularity or elevation above skin surface
Use dermoscopy if available (increases accuracy by 20-30%)
Palpate regional lymph nodes: cervical, axillary, inguinal, epitrochlear
Measure lesion's greatest diameter in mm
Note satellite lesions (skin metastases within 2 cm of primary)
Look for in-transit metastases between primary and nodal basin
Inspect for texture changes in darkly pigmented skin
Examine oral mucosa and genital mucosa
Assess for vitiligo-like depigmentation (halo phenomenon)
Document precise anatomic site for surgical planning
Perform neurologic exam if advanced disease suspected
Assess for lymphedema in the affected limb
Photograph lesion with measurement scale
Perform full abdominal exam (hepatomegaly, ascites for mets)

bedside_scores.name: Breslow Thickness (Histological)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Most important predictor of survival. <1mm: 5-year survival >95%. >4mm: 5-year survival <50%.

subtypes.name: Superficial Spreading Melanoma
subtypes.management: Wide local excision (1-2cm margins depending on Breslow). Sentinel lymph node biopsy if >0.8mm thick. Adjuvant therapy (immunotherapy, targeted) for node-positive or high-risk.

subtypes.name: Nodular Melanoma
subtypes.management: Wide local excision with margins 1-2cm. Sentinel node biopsy. Adjuvant therapy. Close follow-up.

subtypes.name: Lentigo Maligna Melanoma
subtypes.management: Wide local excision or Mohs micrographic surgery for cosmetically sensitive areas. Topical imiquimod for lentigo maligna (in situ). Sentinel node for invasive.

subtypes.name: Acral Lentiginous Melanoma
subtypes.management: Wide excision often requiring amputation of digit. Sentinel node biopsy. Adjuvant therapy. KIT mutation testing for targeted therapy options.

subtypes.name: Mucosal Melanoma
subtypes.management: Surgical resection with negative margins. Checkpoint inhibitors. KIT inhibitors for KIT-mutant cases. Clinical trials.

imaging.name: Excision biopsy (2mm margin) with histology
imaging.indication: definitive diagnosis with Breslow, Clark, ulceration

imaging.name: Brain MRI
imaging.indication: if high risk or neurological symptoms

monitoring: Full skin and lymph node examination

surgical.description: Wide local excision with Breslow-dependent margins
surgical.thick_greater_4mm: 2 cm margins, SLNB, consider adjuvant therapy

adjuvant.step3: Adjuvant radiotherapy for close margins or extensive nodal disease

pathways.treatment: Wide local excision with 0.5cm margins. No SLNB.
Wide local excision with 1cm margins. SLNB optional if high-risk features. No adjuvant therapy.
Wide local excision with 1-2cm margins. SLNB mandatory. Adjuvant immunotherapy if node-positive.
Wide local excision + lymphadenectomy (if palpable nodes). Adjuvant immunotherapy (Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab) or targeted (Dabrafenib + Trametinib if BRAF+). Palliative therapy for M1.

operative_templates.procedure: Wide Local Excision of Cutaneous Melanoma
operative_templates.indication: Primary cutaneous melanoma with histologic confirmation
operative_templates.approach: Elliptical excision with margins based on Breslow thickness
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical, oriented along lymphatics toward nodal basin
steps: Confirm lesion site and planned margins (mark with marker)
Excise full thickness skin and subcutaneous tissue down to fascia
Suture orienting stitch (short = superior, long = lateral)
Send fresh or in formalin with orientation diagram
If tension: rotation flap or skin graft

watchFor: subcutaneous nodules between primary and nodal basin

dischargeCriteria: Adjuvant therapy plan in place (if indicated)
Patient educated on self-examination and sun protection

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Melanoma is a type of skin cancer that arises from the pigment-producing cells in your skin. It can spread to other parts of the body if not treated early. The treatment involves surgically removing the melanoma with a margin of healthy skin around it.
patient_education.warningSigns: Any new or changing mole, bleeding or itching mole, non-healing sore, dark spot under nail or on palm/sole. For follow-up: any new lump, cough, headache, bone pain, weight loss.
patient_education.activity: Normal activities after wound healing. Protect scars from sun for 12 months.
patient_education.diet: Healthy balanced diet. No specific restrictions.
patient_education.medication: As prescribed for adjuvant therapy. Report side effects of immunotherapy (rash, diarrhea, fatigue, hormonal symptoms).
patient_education.followUp: Regular skin checks: every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months. Monthly self-skin examination.

after_discharge.action: Suture removal, wound check, histology review
Clinical review, skin and lymph node exam

when_to_return: Lump in neck, armpit, or groin
Unexplained cough, headache, bone pain, or weight loss

pathophysiologyDetail: Malignant melanoma originates from the malignant transformation of melanocytes, the pigment-producing cells derived from the neural crest that reside in the basal layer of the epidermis. The pathogenesis follows a well-defined molecular progression model. The initiating event is activation of the MAP kinase signaling pathway through mutually exclusive oncogenic mutations in BRAF (40-60% of cutaneous melanomas, primarily V600E), NRAS (15-30%), or NF1 (10-15%), leading to constitutive activation of cell proliferation and survival pathways. BRAF V600E mutation, the most common, results in valine-to-glutamic acid substitution at codon 600, causing a 500-fold increase in kinase activity independent of upstream RAS activation. UV radiation plays a causal role through direct DNA damage (formation of cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and 6-4 photoproducts) and local immunosuppression, with the specific mutation signature (C->T transitions at dipyrimidine sites) detectable in the tumor genome. Melanomas are classified by their growth pattern and anatomic site: superficial spreading (60-70%, most common, horizontal growth phase before vertical invasion), nodular (15-30%, aggressive, vertical growth from onset, worse prognosis), lentigo maligna (5-10%, sun-damaged skin of elderly, indolent), and acral lentiginous (2-8%, palms, soles, subungual, not UV-related, most common type in dark-skinned individuals).

In the Kenyan and East African population, melanoma presents distinct epidemiological and clinical features. The incidence is significantly lower than in Caucasian populations (age-adjusted incidence ~1-2 per 100,000 vs 20-30 per 100,000 in Australia/US), but acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) accounts for a much higher proportion (50-70% vs 2-8% in Caucasians). ALM occurs on the palms, soles, and subungual areas — sites not associated with cumulative UV exposure. The genetic basis differs: KIT mutations (10-20%) and specific NRAS mutations are more common in acral and mucosal melanomas than BRAF mutations. Delayed presentation is the norm in Kenya due to low public awareness, the misconception that dark skin confers immunity to skin cancer, and limited access to dermatology and pathology services. Lesions are frequently diagnosed at advanced stages: at presentation, mean Breslow thickness is 4-8 mm (vs 1-2 mm in high-resource settings), and ulceration is present in 60-70% (vs 20-30%). Regional lymph node involvement at diagnosis is 40-60%, and 5-year survival for Stage III disease in East African series is approximately 15-25% compared to 40-70% in high-resource settings. Pigmented lesions on the feet, subungual areas, and oral mucosa in African patients should be regarded with a high index of suspicion — the 'ABCDE' criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution) are less sensitive in acral melanoma, where any new or changing pigmented lesion on the acral surface deserves biopsy.

The immune system plays a critical role in melanoma surveillance and progression. Melanoma is among the most immunogenic of all cancers, with a high tumor mutational burden (mean 10-50 mutations per megabase) generating numerous neoantigens that can be recognized by cytotoxic T lymphocytes. However, melanoma employs multiple immune evasion strategies: downregulation of MHC Class I expression, secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-beta), recruitment of regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and upregulation of PD-L1 (programmed death ligand 1) which engages PD-1 on T cells and induces T-cell exhaustion. These mechanisms explain both the phenomenon of spontaneous regression (rare, 1-2% of primary melanomas) and the dramatic responses to immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) that have revolutionized the treatment of advanced melanoma in the last decade.

landmarkTrials.trial: KEYNOTE-006 (Pembrolizumab vs Ipilimumab in Advanced Melanoma)
landmarkTrials.population: 834 patients with unresectable Stage III/IV melanoma
landmarkTrials.intervention: Pembrolizumab (10 mg/kg Q2W or Q3W) vs ipilimumab (3 mg/kg Q3W x4)
landmarkTrials.outcome: 12-month OS: 74% pembrolizumab vs 58% ipilimumab (HR 0.63); PFS: 47% vs 26%; fewer high-grade adverse events
landmarkTrials.impact: Established anti-PD-1 therapy as first-line treatment for advanced melanoma, replacing ipilimumab monotherapy
landmarkTrials.reference: Robert C, et al. Pembrolizumab versus ipilimumab in advanced melanoma. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(26):2521-2532.

landmarkTrials.trial: COMBI-AD (Dabrafenib + Trametinib Adjuvant Therapy for BRAF-Mutant Melanoma)
landmarkTrials.population: 870 patients with Stage III BRAF V600-mutant melanoma after complete lymphadenectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Dabrafenib (150 mg BD) + trametinib (2 mg OD) for 12 months vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: 3-year relapse-free survival: 58% vs 40% (HR 0.47); distant metastasis-free survival: 65% vs 50% (HR 0.49)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established targeted adjuvant therapy for BRAF-mutant Stage III melanoma; OS benefit emerging with longer follow-up
landmarkTrials.reference: Long GV, et al. Adjuvant dabrafenib plus trametinib in stage III BRAF-mutated melanoma. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(19):1811-1823.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,934 patients with melanoma and sentinel lymph node metastasis (intermediate thickness 1.2-3.4 mm)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Completion lymph node dissection (CLND) vs observation with serial ultrasound
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in 10-year melanoma-specific survival (86% vs 87%); CLND increased lymphedema (24% vs 6%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Changed practice: CLND no longer recommended for sentinel-positive patients; observation with surveillance is equivalent
landmarkTrials.reference: Faries MB, et al. Completion dissection or observation for sentinel-node metastasis in melanoma. N Engl J Med. 2017;376(23):2211-2222.

landmarkTrials.trial: CheckMate 067 (Nivolumab + Ipilimumab vs Nivolumab vs Ipilimumab)
landmarkTrials.population: 945 patients with previously untreated advanced melanoma; 10-year follow-up reported
landmarkTrials.intervention: Nivolumab (1 mg/kg) + ipilimumab (3 mg/kg) Q3W x4, then nivolumab monotherapy vs nivolumab alone vs ipilimumab alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: 10-year OS: nivolumab+ipilimumab 43%, nivolumab 37%, ipilimumab 21%; median OS: 72.1, 36.9, 19.9 months
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the plateau in survival curves with combination immunotherapy; cured fraction of patients with advanced melanoma
landmarkTrials.reference: Wolchok JD, et al. Long-term outcomes with nivolumab plus ipilimumab or nivolumab alone versus ipilimumab in advanced melanoma (CheckMate 067). Lancet Oncol. 2022;23(8):1015-1025.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: SUSPECT -> BIOPSY -> STAGE -> TREAT -> FOLLOW. SUSPECT: Use the ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution/changing) AND the 'ugly duckling' sign (a lesion that looks different from all others). In African patients, pay particular attention to acral sites (palms, soles, subungual) and mucosal sites (oral, nasal, genital). ANY new or changing pigmented lesion on the foot of an African patient should be biopsied. The 'ABCDE' criteria are less sensitive in acral melanoma; add the 'EFG' criteria for acral lesions: Elevated, Firm, and (progressive) Growth. Subungual melanoma (Hutchinson sign: periungual pigmentation extending from the nail bed to the proximal and lateral nail folds) is frequently misdiagnosed as fungal infection or traumatic hematoma — if a subungual lesion does not grow out with the nail over 6-8 weeks, biopsy is mandatory. BIOPSY: The gold standard is excisional biopsy with 1-2 mm margins (including a small cuff of normal skin and subcutaneous fat down to, but not through, the deep fascia). For cosmetically sensitive areas or large lesions, an incisional biopsy (punch biopsy of the most elevated or darkest area, or the thickest-appearing portion) is acceptable. The biopsy must be oriented to allow subsequent wider local excision. Shave biopsy and cryotherapy are CONTRAINDICATED — they prevent accurate microstaging (Breslow thickness measurement). The pathology report must include: Breslow thickness (mm), Clark level, ulceration (present/absent), mitotic rate (/mm2), margin status, regression, microsatellitosis, and tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte grade. BRAF V600 mutation testing should be performed on all invasive melanomas (Stage IIB or higher). STAGE: Staging requires clinical examination (inspect entire skin, palpate regional lymph nodes), imaging (chest X-ray, LDH, liver ultrasound; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET-CT for Stage >=IIB), and sentinel lymph node biopsy (for intermediate-thickness melanoma 1-4 mm, and select thin melanomas with high-risk features). In Kenya, sentinel lymph node biopsy is available only in specialized centers (Nairobi, Eldoret), and many patients present with clinically palpable nodes warranting fine-needle aspiration or core biopsy. TREAT based on stage: Stage 0 (in situ): wide local excision (0.5-1 cm margin). Stage I-II (localized): wide local excision (1-2 cm margin based on Breslow thickness) with SNB for >=1 mm or high-risk features. Stage III (regional disease): wide local excision + therapeutic lymph node dissection or SNB + adjuvant immunotherapy or targeted therapy. Stage IV (metastatic): immunotherapy (anti-PD-1 +/- anti-CTLA-4) or targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors if BRAF-mutant). In Kenya, access to immunotherapy and targeted therapy is extremely limited (out-of-pocket cost KES 500,000-2,000,000 per month), so palliative care, clinical trials (if available), or referral to centers with oncology access is needed.

managementPearls.pearl: Do NOT shave biopsy, cauterize, or cryotherapy a suspicious pigmented lesion. These methods destroy the specimen, preventing accurate measurement of Breslow thickness — the single most important prognostic factor. Excisional biopsy (2 mm margin, full thickness to fat) or punch biopsy of the thickest area is the standard.

In African patients, acral lentiginous melanoma is the dominant subtype and is frequently missed. Any pigmented lesion on the foot that is changing, bleeding, or ulcerated should be biopsied regardless of size. The classic presentation in Kenya is a large, ulcerated, bleeding lesion on the heel or great toe that has been present for 1-3 years and treated as a 'fungal infection' or 'diabetic ulcer.'

Subungual melanoma (Hutchinson sign) is the great mimicer of the foot. It presents as a longitudinal pigmented band (melanonychia striata) that widens, becomes irregular in color, and develops periungual pigmentation. The differential includes traumatic hematoma (grows out with the nail over 6-8 months) and fungal infection (onychomycosis). A 3-mm punch biopsy through the nail plate into the nail matrix is diagnostic.

For wide local excision of melanoma, the historically recommended margins (5 cm) have been replaced by evidence-based, thickness-dependent margins: In situ: 0.5-1 cm; <1 mm thick: 1 cm; 1-2 mm: 1-2 cm; >2 mm: 2 cm. Wider margins do not improve survival but increase morbidity. The deep margin is the deep fascia itself (excise down to and include the deep fascia).

Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SNB) provides the most powerful prognostic information for intermediate-thickness melanoma. A positive SNB upstages the patient to Stage III, which carries a 40-70% 5-year survival vs 70-90% for SNB-negative patients. SNB should be offered to all patients with melanoma >=1 mm Breslow thickness, and selected patients with <1 mm but high-risk features (ulceration, mitotic rate >=2/mm2, or Clark level IV/V).

operativeNuances.nuance: Wide local excision (WLE) should be oriented along the long axis of the extremity (for limb lesions) or along skin creases (for trunk). The deep margin is the deep fascia — the fascia should be excised en bloc with the specimen. For lesions overlying the parotid, the superficial parotidectomy plane is the deep margin. For lesions near the ear, the perichondrium is the deep margin. Closure options: primary closure (for defects <3-4 cm on trunk or <2 cm on limbs), skin graft (for larger defects or when primary closure would distort anatomy, e.g., eyelid, nose), or local flap (for cosmetically sensitive areas). The surgeon should anticipate the closure before making the incision and have equipment ready for skin grafting if needed.

Sentinel lymph node biopsy technique: on the day of surgery, inject 0.5-1.0 mL of 99m-technetium sulfur colloid (or methylene blue dye if radioisotope unavailable) intradermally around the biopsy site or intact melanoma. Massage for 5 minutes. Use a gamma probe to identify the 'hot' sentinel node(s) in the draining nodal basin. Make a small incision over the hot node, dissect carefully to avoid damaging adjacent structures, and excise all nodes with counts >10% of the hottest node. The nodes are sent for serial sectioning, H&E, and immunohistochemistry (S-100, Melan-A, HMB-45). In Kenya, where radioisotope is rarely available, the blue dye technique (1-2 mL isosulfan blue or methylene blue intradermally) is a practical alternative, though with a slightly lower identification rate (85-90% vs 95-98%).

prognosis.5yearSurvival_StageIV: 15-20% before immunotherapy; 30-50% with modern immunotherapy/targeted therapy
prognosis.kenyanSurvivalData: 5-year survival for Stage III disease in East Africa: 15-25% (delayed presentation, limited access to adjuvant therapy)
prognosis.melanomaSpecificMortality: Melanoma accounts for <1% of skin cancer cases but >65% of skin cancer deaths
prognosis.recurrencePattern: Local recurrence: 2-5% (higher if inadequate margins); in-transit metastasis: 2-10%; regional nodal: 10-30%; distant: 15-40%
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Breslow thickness (the single most important factor), ulceration, mitotic rate, nodal status, age, and access to modern systemic therapy. In Kenya, stage at presentation is the overriding determinant.
